title: Power BI - Reports, Dashboards, And Authoring Conventions
applies_to: Power BI dashboard creation and usage in the service

confirmed_facts.fact: A Power BI report is a multi-perspective view into a semantic model with one or more pages of visualizations, text, and graphics.
Reports can be created in Power BI Desktop and also in the Power BI service, including from semantic models, Excel workbooks, and paginated reports.
Dashboards are a feature of the Power BI service, not Power BI Desktop, and a dashboard is a single canvas made of tiles pinned from reports, semantic models, and other content.
Because dashboards are limited to one page, they differ from reports, which can have many pages and many visuals per page.
Dashboard tiles can link to the underlying report, and dashboards are commonly used to monitor important metrics and navigate into deeper report detail.

implementation_notes: Use reports for analysis, multi-page storytelling, drill-down, and detailed exploration.
Use dashboards for a curated single-page overview of key metrics, alerts, and entry points into deeper reports.
If a user asks for a 'dashboard' but also wants detailed filtering, multiple pages, or extensive analysis, a report is usually the primary deliverable and the dashboard is optional on top.
A good Power BI design flow is semantic model first, then report pages, then optional dashboard pinning in the service.
caveats: Not every report needs a dashboard. Dashboards are most useful for KPI-style monitoring and at-a-glance navigation.
Dashboard capabilities are service-centric, so a Desktop-only workflow cannot fully satisfy a dashboard request.
Pinned dashboard tiles are summaries and can lag behind what a user expects if the semantic model refresh strategy is weak.
